reading.intro: Why a green single-turn score can still ship a broken agent, and what the eval missed.

blocks.text: The eval passed, the agent broke: single-turn versus multi-turn

A single-turn eval hands the model one prompt and grades one response in isolation. It is cheap, it is easy to score, and it tells you whether the model can produce the right answer when everything it needs is already in front of it. A multi-turn agentic task is a different animal. The conversation carries state across several turns, and a single tool call can span turns. The model requests a tool on one turn, receives the tool result on the next, and then must use that result correctly on a later turn. The failure only shows up when state has to survive across turns, which is exactly what a single-turn eval never tests.

blocks.definition: A test that gives the model one prompt and grades one response in isolation, with no state carried in from a previous turn and none expected to survive to a later one. It measures whether the model answers well when everything it needs is present at once.

A task where the conversation runs over several turns and the agent must act, not just answer. State accumulates across turns, and finishing the task depends on the agent using information that arrived on an earlier turn.
Information produced on one turn that the agent must remember and reuse on a later turn. If the agent drops it, the task fails even when every individual turn looks well formed.

A tool interaction that is not resolved within a single turn. The agent requests the tool on one turn, the tool result comes back on the next turn, and the agent has to carry that result forward to a still later turn to finish the job.

blocks.title: Ace the questions, fail the task
blocks.body: Imagine a model that answers every single-turn function-calling question perfectly. Asked to format one call, it formats it. Asked to fill one argument, it fills it. Ship it as an agent and give it a real job. On turn one it calls a lookup tool. On turn two the tool returns an order id. On turn four it has to pass that same order id into a refund tool, but it never carried the value forward, so it invents a blank. The single-turn score was green. The agent is broken. Nothing in the single-turn eval ever asked the model to hold a value across turns.

blocks.text: This is not hypothetical. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories precisely because single-turn scores were not predicting agent behavior. Its multi-turn tasks check backend state across turns, so a model only passes when a value produced early is still used correctly later. The FunctionGemma formatting docs make the same gap explicit from the other side. They state that single-turn and parallel tool calls are supported, while multi-turn and multi-step workflows are not. So the format the model was trained to emit covers the case the single-turn eval measures and stops right at the boundary where multi-turn tasks begin.

The deeper cause was organizational, not just technical. The post-training team and the evals team disagreed over whether multi-turn was even a supported use case, so no one owned the multi-turn eval. An unowned eval is a blind spot, and a blind spot is where a green score hides a broken agent.

reading.summary: A single-turn eval grades one response in isolation, so it can be fully green while the agent still fails a multi-turn task where a value must survive across turns and a tool call spans turns. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories that check backend state across turns, and the FunctionGemma docs confirm single-turn and parallel calls are supported while multi-turn workflows are not. The organizational root cause was that no team owned the multi-turn eval.

diagrams.title: Where the single-turn eval stops seeing
diagrams.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[User asks for a task] --> B[Agent calls a tool]
  B --> C[Tool result returns next turn]
  C --> D{Value carried to a later turn?}
  D -- yes --> E[Task passes]
  D -- no --> F[Task fails, single-turn eval never saw it]
diagrams.takeaway: The single-turn eval only ever inspects one boxed response, so the carry-forward step where multi-turn agents break is outside what it can observe.

audio.script:
Leo: Here is the puzzle. The model aces every single turn function calling question, then we ship it as an agent and it falls over. How does a perfect score hide a broken agent?

Maya: Why does single turn look so clean? Walk me through what it actually measures.

Leo: One prompt in, one response graded in isolation. Everything the model needs sits right there in that one turn, so it is like grading a student on a single flashcard.

Maya: So does that mean a real agent task is different in kind, not just harder?

Leo: Exactly. A multi turn task carries state across turns. Think of it like a relay race where the baton is a value. The model asks for a tool on turn one, the result comes back on turn two, and it has to reuse that same result on turn five.

Maya: How exactly does that break? What changes once a value has to survive across turns?

Leo: The failure only shows up when a token from an earlier turn has to be carried forward. Take the concrete example of a booking id returned on turn two. If the model forgets that row and invents a new id later, the string looks plausible and the world is wrong.

Maya: And why did nobody catch that bug before shipping?

Leo: The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi turn categories that check backend state across turns for exactly this reason. But internally the multi turn eval had no owner, so the gap stayed invisible.

Maya: So does that mean the real failure was organizational, not just technical?

Leo: Right. The post training team and the evals team disagreed over whether multi turn was even in scope. A green single turn score shipped a broken agent because the one eval that mattered was nobody's job.

scene.title: One turn versus many turns
scene.description: A single boxed response is contrasted with a chain of turns where a tool result must be carried forward, showing exactly where the single-turn eval stops seeing.

panels.title: What each eval measures
panels.description: Side by side comparison of the single-turn eval and the multi-turn agentic task and what each one is able to observe.

data.label: single-turn eval
data.value: one prompt, one response graded in isolation

data.label: blind spot
data.value: carry-forward step the single-turn eval never sees

panels.title: The turn chain where it breaks
panels.description: The ordered turns of an agentic task, from the tool call through the returned result to the later turn where the value must be reused.

data.label: turn four
data.value: agent must reuse the order id

cues.label: The puzzle
cues.headline: Green score, broken agent
cues.narration: The model aces every single-turn question, then falls over the moment it runs as an agent.

cues.label: What single-turn measures
cues.headline: One prompt, one graded response
cues.narration: A single-turn eval grades one response in isolation, with everything the model needs already present.
cues.pass: a score that ignores later turns

cues.label: The turn chain
cues.headline: A tool call spans turns
cues.narration: The agent requests a tool on one turn, gets the result on the next, and must reuse it on a later turn.
cues.receive: a tool result on turn two
cues.pass: the value reused on turn four

cues.label: The research grounding
cues.headline: BFCL V3 checks state across turns
cues.narration: The Berkeley leaderboard added multi-turn categories, while the FunctionGemma docs admit multi-turn workflows are not supported.

cues.label: The org root cause
cues.headline: No one owned the eval
cues.narration: Two teams disagreed over whether multi-turn was in scope, so the multi-turn eval had no owner and the gap shipped.

interactive.title: Trace a value across turns
interactive.instructions: Step through a multi-turn task turn by turn. Watch a tool result appear on one turn and decide whether the agent carries it forward to the later turn that needs it. See the task pass when the value survives and fail when it is dropped, the gap a single-turn eval can never catch.

code.prompt: Simulate a tiny multi-turn task. You are given an ordered list of turns. Each turn is a dict. A user message looks like {'role': 'user', 'text': '...'}. A tool call looks like {'role': 'tool_call', 'name': 'lookup'}. A tool result looks like {'role': 'tool_result', 'key': 'order_id', 'value': 'A123'}. The agent's action looks like {'role': 'agent', 'carried': {'order_id': 'A123'}}. Write a checker that decides whether the agent correctly carried a required tool result forward to a later turn (pass) or dropped it (fail). Fill the two TODO helpers, then compose them in carried_forward.
code.starter_code:
def latest_tool_result(turns, key):
    # Scan the turns in order. Return the value from the LAST tool_result whose
    # key matches, or None if no tool_result ever produced that key.
    # TODO: loop over turns, track the matching value, return it.
    pass


def agent_used_value(turns, key):
    # Scan the turns in order. Return what the agent actually carried for key,
    # taken from the LAST agent turn that carried it, or None if it never did.
    # TODO: loop over turns, read turn.get('carried', {}), track key, return it.
    pass


def carried_forward(turns, key):
    expected = latest_tool_result(turns, key)
    actual = agent_used_value(turns, key)
    # The task passes only when a tool result existed AND the agent reused it.
    return expected is not None and actual == expected
constraints: Do not grade any single turn in isolation. The verdict depends on a value produced on one turn and reused on a later turn.
A pass requires two things at once. A tool result must have existed for the key, and the agent must have carried that exact value forward.
If the agent carried nothing or carried the wrong value, the result is a fail even when every individual turn looks well formed.

walkthrough.title: From an ordered list of turns to a pass or fail

steps.title: Find the value the tool produced
steps.detail: Walk the turns in order and remember the value from the last tool_result whose key matches. This is the cross-turn state the agent is supposed to reuse later.
steps.input: turns list and a key like order_id
steps.output: the expected value, or None if no tool result produced it

steps.title: Find what the agent carried
steps.detail: Walk the turns again and read each agent turn's carried dict. Track the value the agent kept for that key so you can compare it against what the tool actually returned.
steps.input: the same turns list and key
steps.output: the value the agent carried, or None if it dropped it

steps.title: Compare and decide
steps.detail: The task passes only when a tool result existed and the agent carried that exact value forward. Any mismatch or missing value is a fail, which is the multi-turn failure a single-turn eval never probes.
steps.output: True on a correct carry forward, False otherwise

io_examples.label: pass
io_examples.explanation: The tool returned A123 and the agent carried A123 forward to its later turn, so the value survived and the task passes.

io_examples.label: fail
io_examples.explanation: The tool returned A123 but the agent carried nothing, so the value was dropped across turns and the task fails.

io_examples.label: wrong value
io_examples.explanation: The agent carried a value forward, but not the one the tool returned, so the carry forward is incorrect and the task fails.

visualization.title: Tool result to carried value to verdict
visualization.description: How a value produced on one turn is compared against what the agent carried to a later turn.

items.label: tool result
items.value: value produced on an earlier turn

items.label: carry forward
items.value: agent keeps or drops it across turns

items.label: verdict
items.value: carried exact value equals tool value ? pass : fail

worked_examples.label: basic
worked_examples.title: Explicit helpers
worked_examples.explanation: latest_tool_result finds the value the tool produced, agent_used_value finds what the agent carried, and carried_forward passes only when both exist and match.

worked_examples.label: concise
worked_examples.title: Inlined checker
worked_examples.explanation: The same logic without helper functions. Track the last tool value and the last carried value, then require that both exist and are equal.

hints.text: The checker never looks at one turn alone. It links a value produced on an earlier turn to what the agent kept for a later turn.
For latest_tool_result, loop over turns and update a variable whenever role is tool_result and key matches, so you keep the last one.
For agent_used_value, read carried = turn.get('carried', {}) on each agent turn and remember carried[key] when key is present.
Use turn.get('role') and turn.get('key') rather than indexing, so a turn missing a field does not raise a KeyError.
carried_forward returns expected is not None and actual == expected, so a dropped value or a wrong value both come back False.

hidden_tests.description: no tool result means nothing to carry
the last tool result and last carried value are the ones compared

questions.prompt: What does a single-turn eval actually measure?
choices: One response to one prompt, graded in isolation with no state carried across turns
Whether a value produced early is reused correctly many turns later
The backend state left behind after a long agentic session
How well two teams agree on the scope of an eval
questions.explanation: A single-turn eval hands the model one prompt and grades one response in isolation, so it never tests whether state survives across turns.

questions.prompt: Which statements about a multi-turn agentic task are true?
choices: The conversation carries state across several turns
A tool call can span turns, with the result arriving on a later turn
The agent may need to reuse an earlier tool result on a later turn
Every turn is graded fully in isolation from the others
questions.explanation: Multi-turn tasks carry state, let tool calls span turns, and require reusing earlier results. Grading each turn in isolation is the single-turn model, not the multi-turn one.

questions.prompt: Which of these are accurate about the research grounding for the single-turn versus multi-turn gap?
choices: The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories that check backend state across turns
The FunctionGemma docs state that multi-turn and multi-step workflows are not supported
The FunctionGemma docs state that single-turn and parallel tool calls are supported
questions.explanation: All three are accurate. BFCL V3 added multi-turn state checks, and the FunctionGemma docs support single-turn and parallel calls while explicitly excluding multi-turn workflows.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are TRUE of a single-turn eval, as opposed to a multi-turn agentic task?
choices: It carries state from an earlier turn into a later turn
It grades a tool call that spans multiple turns
It checks that a value produced early is reused correctly later
questions.explanation: None are true of a single-turn eval. Those are all properties of the multi-turn agentic task. A single-turn eval grades one response in isolation.

questions.prompt: Put the turns of the failing multi-turn task in the order they happen.
items: The user asks the agent to complete a task
The agent calls a lookup tool
The tool returns an order id on a later turn
The agent must reuse the order id but drops it and fails
correct_order: The user asks the agent to complete a task
The agent calls a lookup tool
The tool returns an order id on a later turn
The agent must reuse the order id but drops it and fails

questions.prompt: Explain how a model can pass every single-turn function-calling question and still ship as a broken agent. Include both the technical reason and the organizational reason from the lesson.
questions.actual_answer: Technically, a single-turn eval grades one response in isolation, so it only checks whether the model answers well when everything it needs is already present. A multi-turn agentic task is different because state has to survive across turns and a tool call can span turns. The model requests a tool on one turn, gets the result on the next, and must reuse that result on a later turn. That carry-forward step is exactly what the single-turn eval never exercises, so the model can be perfect on every single-turn question and still drop the value across turns and fail the real task. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories that check backend state across turns for this reason, and the FunctionGemma docs confirm single-turn and parallel calls are supported while multi-turn workflows are not. Organizationally, the post-training team and the evals team disagreed over whether multi-turn was even a supported use case, so no one owned the multi-turn eval. An unowned eval left a blind spot, and the green single-turn score shipped a broken agent.
questions.rubric: Full credit: names the technical reason (single-turn grades one response in isolation while multi-turn needs a value to survive across turns and a tool call to span turns, so the carry-forward step is untested) AND the organizational reason (two teams disagreed on scope, so no one owned the multi-turn eval). Bonus for citing BFCL V3 or the FunctionGemma docs. Partial credit: gives the technical reason or the organizational reason but not both. Low credit: vague or only restates that the eval passed.
